topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a cold, wet stone. My stomach tightened immediately, a familiar clenching sensation that started in my chest and radiated outward. I clicked it open, and the headline swam into view: "Groundbreaking Analysis by Dr. Eleanor Vance." My name wasn't there. My meticulously researched, painstakingly crafted piece, the one I’d poured months of sleepless nights into, was credited to some stranger. A slow burn started in my throat. I reread the attribution, my jaw tightening so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. It wasn't just a mistake; it felt like a deliberate erasure. I stood up, pacing the cramped office, the cheap carpet muffling my hurried steps. Every corner of the room seemed to hold the ghost of the author I’d been, the one who deserved the recognition. I picked up my phone, the screen illuminating my face with a harsh, unforgiving light, and started drafting a reply—a response that felt sharp enough to cut glass.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the cursor blinking mockingly on the published article. The realization hit me with a dull, heavy thud, leaving a strange, hollow ache behind my ribs. It was *their* words, polished and presented as mine. A slow, simmering anger began to build beneath my skin, a restless energy that made my hands tremble slightly as I gripped the edge of my desk. I took a deep, shaky breath, trying to steady the tremor. Who was this person? What kind of carelessness allowed this deception? I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning in protest, and let out a short, humorless exhale. My gaze drifted to the framed diploma on the wall—a testament to years of dedication—and a distinct feeling of injustice washed over me. I didn't move immediately. Instead, I began methodically gathering my notes, stacking them into a neat, accusing pile, a silent declaration that this imbalance of credit would not go unnoticed.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, I felt a sudden, intense heat rise in my cheeks. It wasn't embarrassment; it was something far more corrosive. I read the byline, and the air seemed to evacuate the room. My hands clenched into fists, the knuckles turning white against my skin. I wanted to smash the monitor, to shatter the illusion that I was the architect of this success. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat, a sound devoid of warmth. I walked over to the window, pulling back the blinds just enough to see the indifferent cityscape outside. The world seemed too bright, too cheerful, a stark contrast to the sour taste coating my mouth. I felt a profound sense of being undermined, a deep-seated feeling that someone had stolen my hard-won territory, and the need to reclaim what was rightfully mine became a fierce, burning imperative.
